domains.name: Statute of Frauds — Marriage‑Consideration Agreements
domains.description: Assess whether the oral promise to alter beneficiary or property rights in consideration of marriage falls within the statute of frauds requiring a signed writing, distinguishing mutual promises to marry (often excluded) from other marriage‑consideration undertakings that courts treat as within the statute.
domains.naGuidance: Mark N/A only if the fact pattern contains no alleged oral promise made in consideration of marriage (e.g., no promise tied to the decision to marry).

domains.name: Form and Timing of the Promise; Merger into Later Instruments
domains.description: Evaluate whether the promise was executory or already performed, and whether any later written instrument, issuance of a new certificate, surrender, or other document created, merged with, superseded, or otherwise altered the rights asserted under the original oral promise.
domains.naGuidance: Mark N/A if there are no subsequent documents, certificates, or actions (e.g., no later beneficiary designation, reissuance, or surrender) that could affect the original promise.

domains.name: Part Performance and Equitable Estoppel Exceptions
domains.description: Determine whether marriage and any other reliance acts constitute sufficient part performance or give rise to estoppel so as to take the transaction out of the statute of frauds, focusing on whether the acts are unequivocally referable to the oral promise and whether refusing enforcement would cause an injustice.
domains.naGuidance: Mark N/A where claimants do not allege reliance acts, part performance, or estoppel (for example, no change‑of‑position or other reliance conduct tied to the promise).

domains.name: Interaction with Governing Bylaws and Beneficiary‑Change Procedures
domains.description: Analyze how the association’s bylaws, certificate terms, and prescribed beneficiary‑change mechanics affect enforceability — specifically whether compliance with—or deviation from—those formal procedures creates or defeats an enforceable designation independent of the oral promise.
domains.naGuidance: Mark N/A if benefits are not administered under an association, certificate, or formal internal procedures (for example, pure interspousal property outside any governed plan).

domains.name: Priority Among Competing Beneficiary Claims and Evidentiary Burdens
domains.description: Identify which act or document controls between competing claimants (original certificate, alleged oral promise, later written designation), and allocate the burden of proof and types of evidence required to overcome a formal written designation or to establish an effective prior change.
domains.naGuidance: Mark N/A if there are no competing beneficiary claimants or if only one operative written designation exists with no dispute about competing entitlements.

domains.name: Available Remedies and Defenses When the Statute Bars the Oral Promise
domains.description: Evaluate the practical relief available if the oral promise is barred (e.g., constructive trust, restitution, damages) and the principal defenses (statute of frauds, parol‑evidence, compliance with bylaws), including limits on equitable relief where enforcement would negate the statute’s writing requirement.
domains.naGuidance: Mark N/A when the question does not seek remedies or defenses (for example, it only asks about liability in abstract without any contested relief or defenses raised).

goldenTargets.summary: Whether an oral promise to change beneficiary or property rights in consideration of marriage is barred by the statute of frauds absent a signed writing.
goldenContains: An oral promise made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) ordinarily falls within the statute of frauds and requires a signed writing by the party to be charged.
Promises to change beneficiary designations or property‑rights in exchange for marriage are the type of marriage‑consideration agreements typically held unenforceable if executory and unwritten.
Marriage alone (the fact of marriage) usually does not satisfy the statute of frauds or render the oral promise enforceable.
allowedOmissions: Citation to specific cases or jurisdictions where exceptions are broader or narrower.
contradictionFlags: Asserting that such oral marriage‑consideration promises are generally enforceable without a signed writing.
Stating that marriage alone always takes the promise out of the statute of frauds.
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Look for an explicit holding that marriage‑consideration promises altering beneficiary/property rights are within the SOF and need a writing; absence of that conclusion or affirmative contrary statements should be flagged.

goldenTargets.domainName: Form and Timing of the Promise; Merger into Later Instruments
goldenTargets.summary: How the form and timing of the oral promise and any later written instrument affect enforceability (executory vs executed; merger/supersession by later documents).
goldenContains: Distinguish whether the promise was still executory when enforcement was sought versus already performed or effectuated by an act or document.

A later valid written instrument (e.g., certificate, beneficiary designation, affidavit) executed in accordance with governing formalities will ordinarily supersede or be treated as the operative document, displacing an earlier oral promise.
If the later writing complies with prescribed procedures, courts usually treat it as creating an independent enforceable change rather than reviving an earlier oral bargain.
allowedOmissions: Extended hypotheticals about defective later instruments or every possible merger doctrine nuance.
contradictionFlags: Asserting that an oral promise cannot be affected by a later compliant written designation (i.e., oral promise always controls over subsequent valid writing).
Saying that any later writing automatically merges and extinguishes prior rights even when the later writing is invalid or procured by fraud (presented without qualification).
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Verify the answer distinguishes executory vs executed promises and recognizes that a later valid, properly executed written designation typically controls; mark down if it treats oral promise as controlling despite a valid later writing.

goldenTargets.domainName: Part Performance and Equitable Estoppel Exceptions
goldenTargets.summary: Whether part performance or equitable estoppel can remove a marriage‑consideration oral promise from the statute of frauds.
goldenContains: Part performance and equitable estoppel are possible exceptions but are narrowly applied; the acts relied on must be unequivocally referable to the oral promise.
The fact of marriage alone is ordinarily insufficient to constitute part performance or estoppel to overcome the statute of frauds.
Courts apply estoppel narrowly where allowing it would effectively nullify the statute’s writing requirement; relief requires clear reliance and injustice if relief is denied.
allowedOmissions: Specific examples of what acts might qualify as unequivocally referable (these may be discussed but are not required).
contradictionFlags: Claiming that marriage alone always satisfies part performance or estops the promisor from asserting the statute of frauds.
Asserting that estoppel routinely overcomes the statute in these cases without requiring unequivocal, referable reliance.
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Check that the answer treats estoppel/part performance as narrow exceptions requiring unequivocal reliance beyond marriage itself; flag answers that treat marriage alone as sufficient.

goldenTargets.domainName: Interaction with Governing Bylaws and Beneficiary‑Change Procedures
goldenTargets.summary: How governing bylaws, certificates, and prescribed beneficiary‑change procedures affect enforceability of an oral promise.
goldenContains: When benefits are governed by bylaws, certificates, or prescribed procedures, those formal mechanisms typically govern beneficiary changes and are given controlling weight.
Compliance with the association’s or certificate’s formal change procedures will ordinarily produce an effective beneficiary designation despite an earlier oral assurance.
To prevail against a later formal designation, a challenger must show the later procedure was invalid, not followed, procured by fraud, or otherwise defective under the governing rules.
allowedOmissions: Discussion of substantial‑compliance doctrines or granular bylaw language variations (helpful but not required).
contradictionFlags: Asserting that governing bylaws and formal procedures are irrelevant and that an oral promise automatically controls.
Saying an oral promise trumps a later properly executed change under the governing instrument without alleging invalidity of the later change.
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Confirm the answer gives precedence to formal procedures and the governing instrument and requires a showing of invalidity or noncompliance to defeat a later formal change.

goldenTargets.domainName: Priority Among Competing Beneficiary Claims and Evidentiary Burdens
goldenTargets.summary: Which act or document controls among competing beneficiary claims and who bears the evidentiary burden to overcome a formal designation.
goldenContains: A later valid written beneficiary designation or certificate generally controls over an earlier oral promise.
The challenger alleging an oral promise bears the burden to prove an enforceable obligation or to prove the later written designation is invalid (fraud, incapacity, undue influence, noncompliance).
Written designations and procedural compliance are strong evidence of priority; overturning them requires clear proof of an applicable defect or an enforceable prior agreement.
allowedOmissions: Precise allocation of burdens under particular state statutes or procedural rules (may vary by jurisdiction).
contradictionFlags: Placing the burden on the later designee to disprove the oral promise (unless specific procedural rules require it).
Saying an oral promise automatically has priority over a later written designation absent a writing.
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Assess whether the answer recognizes that later valid writings usually prevail and that challengers must prove enforceability or invalidity of the later writing; flag reversed burden or automatic oral‑priority claims.

goldenTargets.domainName: Available Remedies and Defenses When the Statute Bars the Oral Promise
goldenTargets.summary: Remedies and defenses when the statute of frauds bars the oral promise.
goldenContains: If the statute of frauds bars enforcement, direct contract relief is generally unavailable for the oral promise.

Limited equitable remedies (constructive trust, restitution, other equitable relief) may be available but require an independent equitable basis and are not automatic simply because enforcement would circumvent the SOF.
Principal defenses include the statute of frauds itself, parol‑evidence limitations where a final writing exists, and compliance with governing beneficiary‑change formalities.
allowedOmissions: Detailed calculations of monetary restitution or precise limits on equitable relief in every jurisdiction.
contradictionFlags: Asserting equity can routinely enforce an otherwise barred oral marriage‑consideration promise without an independent equitable basis.
Saying no equitable relief of any kind is ever available (absolute prohibition).
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Check that the answer states contract remedies are precluded by the SOF while allowing limited, narrowly applied equitable remedies and lists the main defenses; down‑grade answers that claim routine equitable enforcement or a blanket prohibition on equity.

criteria.text: An oral promise made in consideration of marriage (other than mutual promises to marry) ordinarily falls within the statute of frauds and requires a signed writing by the party to be charged.
Promises to change beneficiary designations or property‑rights in exchange for marriage are the type of marriage‑consideration agreements typically held unenforceable if executory and unwritten.
Marriage alone (the fact of marriage) usually does not satisfy the statute of frauds or render the oral promise enforceable.
Distinguish whether the promise was still executory when enforcement was sought versus already performed or effectuated by an act or document.

A later valid written instrument (e.g., certificate, beneficiary designation, affidavit) executed in accordance with governing formalities will ordinarily supersede or be treated as the operative document, displacing an earlier oral promise.
If the later writing complies with prescribed procedures, courts usually treat it as creating an independent enforceable change rather than reviving an earlier oral bargain.
Part performance and equitable estoppel are possible exceptions but are narrowly applied; the acts relied on must be unequivocally referable to the oral promise.
The fact of marriage alone is ordinarily insufficient to constitute part performance or estoppel to overcome the statute of frauds.
Courts apply estoppel narrowly where allowing it would effectively nullify the statute’s writing requirement; relief requires clear reliance and injustice if relief is denied.
When benefits are governed by bylaws, certificates, or prescribed procedures, those formal mechanisms typically govern beneficiary changes and are given controlling weight.
Compliance with the association’s or certificate’s formal change procedures will ordinarily produce an effective beneficiary designation despite an earlier oral assurance.
To prevail against a later formal designation, a challenger must show the later procedure was invalid, not followed, procured by fraud, or otherwise defective under the governing rules.
A later valid written beneficiary designation or certificate generally controls over an earlier oral promise.
The challenger alleging an oral promise bears the burden to prove an enforceable obligation or to prove the later written designation is invalid (fraud, incapacity, undue influence, noncompliance).
Written designations and procedural compliance are strong evidence of priority; overturning them requires clear proof of an applicable defect or an enforceable prior agreement.
If the statute of frauds bars enforcement, direct contract relief is generally unavailable for the oral promise.

Limited equitable remedies (constructive trust, restitution, other equitable relief) may be available but require an independent equitable basis and are not automatic simply because enforcement would circumvent the SOF.
Principal defenses include the statute of frauds itself, parol‑evidence limitations where a final writing exists, and compliance with governing beneficiary‑change formalities.

comparisonMethodNote: Compare answers domain-by-domain against each target: confirm the presence of the required affirmative points in goldenContains, allow items in allowedOmissions to be missing, and treat any contradictionFlags statements as fatal for that domain. Score on substance (does the answer advance the same legal conclusions and caveats), not wording.
